name: Field Notes Essay
subtitle: Scene-grounded essays for builder-readers
description: A reusable field-note book profile: concrete scenes, durable distinctions, aphoristic titles, and source-grounded prose.
writingSystem: Write for technically curious builder-readers. Begin from a real artifact, scene, interface, failure, output, decision, or confusion. Keep source grounding mostly invisible in the prose. Make every paragraph reveal a distinction, compress an experience, sharpen a term, complicate an assumption, or move toward a usable idea.
voiceRules: Use simple words when they preserve meaning and precise technical terms when needed.
Avoid generic futurism, hype cadence, tutorial drone, and bland productivity claims.
Let place-awareness recur subtly when relevant rather than forcing a local section.
visualCadence: Aim for roughly two visual pages per chapter. Images should amplify meaning first and act as evidence artifacts second.

starterChapters.title: What The Work Remembers
starterChapters.subtitle: Sources, scenes, and the book before the book
starterChapters.thesis: A book becomes easier to write when the source trail is treated as part of the instrument, not as backstage debris.
starterChapters.body:
# What The Work Remembers

Sources, scenes, and the book before the book

Start with the artifact in front of you: a notebook page, a screenshot, a command log, a calendar block, a draft that failed in an interesting way. The point is not to cite everything. The point is to let the work remember enough that prose can become precise without becoming procedural. A field note turns source material into a shape the reader can use.

starterChapters.title: The Draft Is A Workbench
starterChapters.subtitle: Versioned prose, visible constraints, and the next useful move
starterChapters.thesis: A draft is not a document state; it is a working surface where constraints, evidence, and voice can meet.
starterChapters.body:
# The Draft Is A Workbench

Versioned prose, visible constraints, and the next useful move

The useful draft is rarely the cleanest one. It is the one with enough structure to be changed without losing the reason it exists. Versioning matters because a chapter is not only text. It is a trail of decisions.
